sentence: Subjective:
HPI: The patient, Wayne, presents with a sore on his right foot that has not been healing for about two to three weeks.
He reports that he initially developed a blister due to a new pair of shoes, which progressed to a wound that is now about 1 by 2 inches in size.
He denies any trauma to the foot and has been putting a band-aid on it.
He also denies any symptoms such as fever, chills, drainage, or pain from the wound.
However, he mentions that the wound sticks to his sock when he takes it off.
He has a history of diabetes, with a last HbA1c of 8.1.
Past medical history: The patient has a history of diabetes, and he used to see a podiatrist but had to switch due to their relocation.
Review of systems: The patient denies any other symptoms.
Current medications: [NOT MENTIONED]
Objective:
Vital signs: The patient's temperature is 98.1°F, heart rate is 72, respirations are 16, and blood pressure is 110/65.

Physical exam: The patient has a 1 by 2 inch circular wound on the dorsal aspect of the lateral right foot, just proximal to the right fifth MTP joint, with yellow slough and minimal granulation tissue.
There is no surrounding erythema or cellulitis, no fluid collection, no necrosis, and no odor.
The vascular exam shows palpable bilateral femoral and popliteal pulses, but no palpable DP or PT pulses.
Doppler signs are present.
Test Results: The patient's right foot X-ray shows no evidence of osteomyelitis.
Assessment / Problem List:
Assessment: The patient has a diabetic foot ulcer on the right foot, with a wound that is not healing due to inadequate blood supply.
Problem list: Diabetic foot ulcer; suspected impaired wound healing; unknown status of diabetes.
Plan:
I prescribed collagenase ointment to be applied to the wound once daily and covered with a dry sterile dressing.
I will perform a debridement in the office to remove dead tissue.
I will order an ankle-brachial index (ABI) to determine the blood supply to the foot.
I will refer the patient to a vascular specialist if the ABI indicates inadequate blood supply for wound healing.
I will also refer the patient to a diabetic consultant to discuss ways to improve his diabetes control.
I advised the patient to wear a surgical shoe to take pressure off the area.
Follow-up: The patient will be seen again in two weeks.
